raw_response: {"rating": -3.5, "lean": "Left", "explanation": "This is a news report that frames EPA website changes as an assault on scientific integrity, relying almost exclusively on critical voices while characterizing the administration's pro-fossil fuel policies in negative terms. The accumulation of framing choices, source selection, and editorial characterizations consistently aligns with left-leaning values around climate science and environmental protection, supporting a Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author describes the administration's energy goals as trying to 'supercharge' oil and gas production and 'resurrect' the coal industry — both verb choices carry negative connotations (recklessness, anachronism) rather than neutral policy language like 'expand' or 'revive'; (2) Source selection is structurally one-sided: the author quotes three climate scientists and a former Biden EPA official, all critical of the changes, while the EPA spokesperson's rebuttal is presented briefly and framed dismissively — the author does not seek or include any scientific or policy voice supportive of the administration's position; (3) The author characterizes the 2009 endangerment finding as a 'landmark scientific finding' in their own prose, an editorial judgment that elevates its authority before describing the administration's effort to repeal it; (4) The author frames the Energy Department report through the lens of its critics alone, describing it as written by 'longtime climate contrarians' who were 'cherry-picking evidence' — these are characterizations the author adopts as their own narrative rather than attributing to a specific source; (5) The phrase 'the bigger problem' is introduced in the author's own transition language, subtly directing the reader toward the framing preferred by the critical sources; (6) The former Biden EPA official Joe Goffman is given the final substantive word in the piece, and his framing — that the administration needed to 'wipe out' scientific information — is allowed to stand as a concluding characterization with no counterpoint; (7) The article omits any neutral scientific or legal perspective on the complexity of the endangerment finding repeal, presenting repeal solely as an ideologically motivated attack on science rather than as a contested regulatory and legal question."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report that frames EPA website changes as an assault on scientific integrity, relying almost exclusively on critical voices while characterizing the administration's pro-fossil fuel policies in negative terms. The accumulation of framing choices, source selection, and editorial characterizations consistently aligns with left-leaning values around climate science and environmental protection, supporting a Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author describes the administration's energy goals as trying to 'supercharge' oil and gas production and 'resurrect' the coal industry — both verb choices carry negative connotations (recklessness, anachronism) rather than neutral policy language like 'expand' or 'revive'; (2) Source selection is structurally one-sided: the author quotes three climate scientists and a former Biden EPA official, all critical of the changes, while the EPA spokesperson's rebuttal is presented briefly and framed dismissively — the author does not seek or include any scientific or policy voice supportive of the administration's position; (3) The author characterizes the 2009 endangerment finding as a 'landmark scientific finding' in their own prose, an editorial judgment that elevates its authority before describing the administration's effort to repeal it; (4) The author frames the Energy Department report through the lens of its critics alone, describing it as written by 'longtime climate contrarians' who were 'cherry-picking evidence' — these are characterizations the author adopts as their own narrative rather than attributing to a specific source; (5) The phrase 'the bigger problem' is introduced in the author's own transition language, subtly directing the reader toward the framing preferred by the critical sources; (6) The former Biden EPA official Joe Goffman is given the final substantive word in the piece, and his framing — that the administration needed to 'wipe out' scientific information — is allowed to stand as a concluding characterization with no counterpoint; (7) The article omits any neutral scientific or legal perspective on the complexity of the endangerment finding repeal, presenting repeal solely as an ideologically motivated attack on science rather than as a contested regulatory and legal question.

explanation: This is a news report that frames EPA website changes as an assault on scientific integrity, relying almost exclusively on critical voices while characterizing the administration's pro-fossil fuel policies in negative terms. The accumulation of framing choices, source selection, and editorial characterizations consistently aligns with left-leaning values around climate science and environmental protection, supporting a Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author describes the administration's energy goals as trying to 'supercharge' oil and gas production and 'resurrect' the coal industry — both verb choices carry negative connotations (recklessness, anachronism) rather than neutral policy language like 'expand' or 'revive'; (2) Source selection is structurally one-sided: the author quotes three climate scientists and a former Biden EPA official, all critical of the changes, while the EPA spokesperson's rebuttal is presented briefly and framed dismissively — the author does not seek or include any scientific or policy voice supportive of the administration's position; (3) The author characterizes the 2009 endangerment finding as a 'landmark scientific finding' in their own prose, an editorial judgment that elevates its authority before describing the administration's effort to repeal it; (4) The author frames the Energy Department report through the lens of its critics alone, describing it as written by 'longtime climate contrarians' who were 'cherry-picking evidence' — these are characterizations the author adopts as their own narrative rather than attributing to a specific source; (5) The phrase 'the bigger problem' is introduced in the author's own transition language, subtly directing the reader toward the framing preferred by the critical sources; (6) The former Biden EPA official Joe Goffman is given the final substantive word in the piece, and his framing — that the administration needed to 'wipe out' scientific information — is allowed to stand as a concluding characterization with no counterpoint; (7) The article omits any neutral scientific or legal perspective on the complexity of the endangerment finding repeal, presenting repeal solely as an ideologically motivated attack on science rather than as a contested regulatory and legal question.
